fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this native bridge adapter thing for the Neon bindings project. Basically the idea is that native Rust code should be able to hand values back and forth with JavaScript without developers having to write all that ugly glue code every single time. Think of it like that interop layer we did for the login module — same vibe but for the full value spectrum: numbers, strings, arrays, objects, buffers, dates, errors, async stuff, all of it.

The way it works is commands come in as JSON on stdin and the results go to stdout in a key=value line format. Each command has an operation and a scenario field at minimum, sometimes extra fields depending on what's being tested. The adapter needs to handle all the primitive types, binary memory views, boxed stateful objects, async callbacks and promises, and error categories without leaking internal Rust exception names to the caller.

One thing that tripped us up last time is that error categories need to be normalized — callers should never see raw implementation names. Same goes for script evaluation failures, there are two different failure modes there that need to be reported differently.

Also the binary memory stuff has some tricky overlap/borrow behavior that needs to fail gracefully. Users have been complaining about crashes at the boundary when they try to do concurrent reads and writes on the same buffer region, so that needs to be locked down properly.

Please follow the same patterns from the existing test fixtures in rcb_tests.

trigger_keywords: how should undefined and null be printed
what is the output format for undefined or null values
how do we render primitive singletons in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Undefined must be rendered as the literal string 'undefined' and null as the literal string 'null' in key=value output lines. Do not use empty strings, 'None', or any other representation. See feature1_module_exports.json: expected output 'undefined=undefined\nnull=null\n'.

trigger_keywords: how do script evaluation errors differ
what is the difference between a syntax error and a runtime error in script eval
how should evaluate_script failures be reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly two distinct failure modes for script evaluation. A runtime exception thrown during execution must be reported as 'error=script_exception' followed by a 'message=<text>' line. A parse/syntax failure must be reported as 'error=script_syntax' with NO message line and must NOT leak any runtime exception class names. See feature3_string_scripts.json for the contract.

trigger_keywords: what error categories are allowed for error objects
how should rust errors be normalized for the caller
what names should error types use in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Error categories must be normalized to stable string labels: 'generic_error' for generic errors, 'type_error' for type errors, and 'range_error' for range errors. Never expose Rust or internal exception type names. Type and range errors must additionally print 'base_error=true'. See feature10_errors.json.

trigger_keywords: what happens when binary buffer regions overlap
how should overlapping mutable borrows be handled
what is the expected failure for invalid buffer regions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Invalid memory region accesses and overlapping mutable borrow attempts must fail gracefully with a normalized error category output rather than panicking or crashing. The output must use the same normalized error category contract as error objects (e.g., 'range_error' for out-of-bounds regions). See feature11_binary_memory.json hidden constraints.

trigger_keywords: what does the binary memory read_write scenario output look like
what specific byte values are expected for array_buffer_read_write
what is the exact output for buffer_read_write
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For 'array_buffer_read_write': output must be 'read_values=47,133,9,88888888
written_bytes=3,42,127,255
borrow_read_values=49,135,11,237
borrow_written_bytes=43,100,22,243
'. For 'buffer_read_write': output must be 'lock_read=147,113,109,189
borrow_read=149,224,70,229
lock_written=6,61,45,216
borrow_written=16,100,232,55
'. These are fixed test vectors — the implementation must produce exactly these values.

trigger_keywords: how should callback invocation results be formatted
what is the output for zero argument function calls
how are heterogeneous argument arrays printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For the 'callback_invocation' scenario: zero-args must output '-Infinity' (not 'NaN' or empty), heterogeneous args must be printed as a JSON array literal e.g. '[1,"hello",true]'. The full expected output is: 'direct_call=17
idiomatic_call=17
zero_args=-Infinity
one_arg=1
two_args=2
three_args=3
four_args=4
heterogeneous=[1,"hello",true]
'. See feature8_function_calls.json.

trigger_keywords: how is a date printed in output
what format should UTC date strings use
what happens with an invalid date timestamp
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Valid dates created from millisecond timestamps must be printed as 'millis=<ms>\nutc=<RFC 2822 UTC string>\n', e.g. 'utc=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:31 GMT'. Invalid dates must print the timestamp as 'NaN' and include an 'is_valid=false' line. See feature9_dates.json.

trigger_keywords: how should the binary predicate checks be ordered in output
what is the exact output for binary_predicates scenario
how do buffer vs array_buffer predicates differ
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The 'binary_predicates' scenario must output exactly: 'array_buffer=true
data_view=false
uint8_array_as_array_buffer=false
byte_buffer=true
uint8_array_as_buffer=true
array_buffer_as_buffer=false
uint32_array=true
uint16_array_as_uint32=false
'. A Node.js Buffer IS a Uint8Array and reports true for buffer checks but false for ArrayBuffer checks. A raw ArrayBuffer is false for buffer checks. See feature7_type_predicates.json.

trigger_keywords: how should the core logic be separated from stdin/stdout
can the adapter parsing be in the same file as business logic
what is the required file structure for this project
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON stdin/stdout I/O adapter must be strictly separated from the core domain logic. The execution adapter translates JSON commands into idiomatic method calls on the core system — the core must never directly depend on JSON parsing or stdout. Multi-file structure under src/ is required since this is a complex domain with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, feature modules, output formatting).

trigger_keywords: what large integer value is used in created_numbers
what float values are expected for the numbers scenario
what is the expected output for created_numbers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The 'created_numbers' scenario must output exactly: 'integer=9000
large_integer=4294967296
negative_integer=-9000
float=1.4747
negative_float=-1.4747
'. The large integer 4294967296 is 2^32 and must be represented as-is (it is within the safe float64 range). See feature2_numbers.json.

trigger_keywords: how should async results be reported
what is the output for the lazy_add async scenario
how do promise resolutions get printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the 'lazy_add' async scenario the output must be 'result=3\n'. Async callbacks and promises must resolve before the adapter writes output — the adapter must await resolution. Rejections must use normalized error category output consistent with the error contract. See feature13_async_results.json.

trigger_keywords: how does the boxed state greet scenario work
what output is expected for ref_greet vs greet
how should opaque stateful objects be handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both 'greet' and 'ref_greet' scenarios for the boxed operation must produce identical output: 'name=<name>\ngreeting=Hello, <name>!\n'. The 'ref_greet' variant uses a borrowed reference rather than ownership transfer but the observable output is the same. Borrow/type check failures must use normalized error categories. See feature12_boxed_state.json.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same vibe as that interop layer we did for the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Strict Separation of Concerns constraint from the Architecture section: the JSON I/O adapter must be decoupled from the core domain logic, mirroring the adapter pattern described in the PRD's engineering constraints section. See start.md Architecture constraint #2.

context_pointers.vague_reference: error categories need to be normalized — callers should never see raw implementation names
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 10 error normalization contract: generic_error, type_error, range_error labels in output; plus Feature 3 script evaluation failure modes: error=script_exception with message vs error=script_syntax without message. See feature10_errors.json and feature3_string_scripts.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: crashes at the boundary when they try to do concurrent reads and writes on the same buffer region
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 11 binary memory overlapping mutable borrow scenario: must produce a normalized range_error category output rather than panicking, covering the hidden test cases in feature11_binary_memory.json beyond the three public cases shown.

context_pointers.vague_reference: please follow the same patterns from the existing test fixtures in rcb_tests
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All output must follow strict key=value newline-delimited format with no extra whitespace, using the exact field names and value representations shown in each rcb_tests/public_test_cases/featureN_*.json file.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have a specific answer for that detail off the top of my head — you'll want to look at the test fixture files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected outputs, and cross-reference with the PRD in start.md for the architectural constraints. The test cases are the ground truth for observable behavior.

feature10_errors.json.description: Error objects preserve their domain category and message, thrown messages are reported through a normalized error contract, and failed conversions can be rendered as domain text.

cases.expected_output:
category=type_error
message=Type error? From Rust?!
base_error=true

feature11_binary_memory.json.description: Binary memory operations expose byte lengths, byte contents, view metadata, copy behavior, and normalized failures for invalid or overlapping regions.

feature12_boxed_state.json.description: Opaque stateful values can be created, greeted, mutated, and protected by dynamic borrow/type checks reported with neutral error categories.

feature13_async_results.json.description: Asynchronous callbacks, promises, and worker-backed computations resolve to numeric results or reject with normalized exception categories.

feature1_module_exports.json.description: Values exported at module initialization are visible with the expected primitive, number, object, and callable behavior.

feature2_numbers.json.description: Numeric values crossing the native boundary preserve integer, large integer, floating point, and negative-number behavior as observed by callers.

feature3_string_scripts.json.description: String values can be returned, evaluated as source text, and reported with normalized script error categories when evaluation fails.

feature4_arrays.json.description: Array outputs preserve length and indexed content, and reading the first element returns either that value or an undefined sentinel for an empty array.

feature5_objects.json.description: Object outputs preserve own properties, object immutability operations affect later mutation/deletion attempts, own-property enumeration excludes inherited and symbolic keys, and method invocation uses the receiver object.

feature6_value_stringification.json.description: Values are converted to strings using the host value conversion semantics observable at the boundary.

feature7_type_predicates.json.description: Runtime predicates report the observable kind of values, and strict identity comparison distinguishes equal primitives from distinct object references.

cases.expected_output:
array_buffer=true
data_view=false
uint8_array_as_array_buffer=false
byte_buffer=true
uint8_array_as_buffer=true
array_buffer_as_buffer=false
uint32_array=true
uint16_array_as_uint32=false

feature8_function_calls.json.description: Functions crossing the boundary remain callable, receive the expected arguments and receiver, support construction, expose argument counts/presence, and can capture thrown values as data.

cases.expected_output:
direct_call=17
idiomatic_call=17
zero_args=-Infinity
one_arg=1
two_args=2
three_args=3
four_args=4
heterogeneous=[1,"hello",true]

feature9_dates.json.description: Date values can be created from millisecond timestamps, converted back to timestamps, and classified as valid or invalid with invalid values represented as NaN.
